ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for Oil Rice (油饭). Mapped to glutinous rice dish as the closest defensible parent class. FoodOn does not have a specific entry for this Singaporean braised glutinous rice preparation.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00GJ (Rice dishes) used as the closest defensible category. No specific FoodEx2 code exists for Oil Rice / 油饭 as a distinct Singaporean preparation.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Oil Rice as a specific food concept. SNOMED CT food ontology does not cover this dish at the required specificity.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving of Oil Rice is approximately 200g (one rice bowl), as commonly consumed at hawker centres and food courts in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Oil Rice (油饭) — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database for Oil Rice (油饭), CR ID G00010711. All per-100g values are taken from the canonical HPB record with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat data not reported by HPB for this entry; estimated from USDA FoodData Central analogous glutinous rice dish (SR Legacy) and documented accordingly.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Glutinous Rice and Rice-Based Dishes — University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Singaporean Oil Rice (油饭) in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 72 is estimated from glutinous rice (sticky rice), which has a published GI of approximately 70–75 in the University of Sydney GI Database and the Atkinson et al. 2008 international tables. Oil Rice is predominantly glutinous rice with braised toppings; the fat and protein from pork belly and dried shrimp may modestly lower the effective GI, but glutinous rice dominates the glycaemic response. GI of 72 (high) is used as the best defensible analogous estimate. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (72 × 50.38) / 100 = 36.3 for a 200g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 231mg/100g (462mg per 200g serving) is in the borderline-to-moderate range for hypertension management; potassium at 327mg/100g partially offsets sodium load.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Oil Rice to a half-bowl portion (100g), request reduced soy sauce and oyster sauce during preparation, and avoid adding further condiments such as chili sauce or additional soy sauce at the table to keep sodium intake within the recommended 2000mg/day limit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Glutinous rice base confers a high glycemic index (estimated GI ~72); dietary fibre at 3.7g/100g partially moderates the glycaemic response but does not fully offset the high-GI carbohydrate load of 25.2g/100g.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit Oil Rice to a half-bowl serving (approximately 100g), pair it with non-starchy vegetables and a protein source to blunt postprandial glucose rise, and monitor blood glucose response given the high-GI glutinous rice base.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate fat content (4.21g/100g) with estimated saturated fat of approximately 1.3g/100g from pork belly; sodium at 231mg/100g is a secondary concern for cardiovascular risk; iron at 2.55mg/100g and dietary fibre at 3.7g/100g are beneficial.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should choose a reduced-portion serving of Oil Rice (100g), request preparation with less pork belly and more mushroom or tofu to lower saturated fat intake, and pair with vegetables to increase dietary fibre and support lipid management.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pork belly contributes pro-inflammatory saturated fat and arachidonic acid (omega-6); dried shrimp and soy sauce contribute sodium which may worsen joint swelling; mushrooms and sesame oil provide anti-inflammatory beta-glucans and MUFA/PUFA respectively.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should request a mushroom-only or chicken-based version of Oil Rice to reduce pro-inflammatory saturated fat and arachidonic acid, and ask for reduced soy sauce and oyster sauce to minimise sodium-driven joint inflammation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Oil Rice (油饭, Yóu Fàn) is a traditional Singaporean-Chinese braised glutinous rice dish commonly found at hawker centres, food courts, and festive occasions across Singapore. It is prepared by cooking glutinous (sticky) rice with a rich braising sauce made from soy sauce, oyster sauce, sesame oil, and five-spice powder, then combining it with braised toppings that typically include pork belly, dried shrimp, shiitake mushrooms, shallots, and garlic. The result is a dense, savoury, and aromatic rice dish with a chewy, sticky texture and deep umami flavour from the combination of soy-based sauces and dried seafood. Oil Rice is culturally significant in Chinese-Singaporean cuisine and is frequently served at celebratory events, full-moon celebrations, and as a hearty lunch or dinner option. Nutritionally, per 100g it provides 164 kcal, 6.5g protein, 4.21g fat, 25.19g carbohydrates, 3.7g dietary fibre, and 231mg sodium. The dietary fibre content at 3.7g/100g is notable for a rice dish and comes primarily from the mushrooms and glutinous rice bran. The high glycemic index (estimated ~72) of the glutinous rice base means postprandial blood glucose rises rapidly; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size and pair with non-starchy vegetables. The sodium content of 231mg/100g warrants caution for those managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. Iron at 2.55mg/100g and potassium at 327mg/100g are clinically meaningful contributions. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those requiring halal-certified food without specific preparation modifications.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (CR ID G00010711) with a canonical confidence of 0.95. The HPB is the primary approved source for Singaporean dishes. Saturated fat and fatty acid breakdown estimated from USDA FoodData Central analogous glutinous rice dish and documented. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database glutinous rice data as no direct measurement exists for this specific dish.
notes: Saturated fat (1.3g/100g), monounsaturated_fat_g (1.7g/100g), and polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.9g/100g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for glutinous rice dish with pork and mushroom toppings, as HPB does not report fatty acid breakdown for this entry. Trans fat set to 0.0 as no trans fats are expected in this traditional preparation. Cholesterol reported as 0.0mg/100g per HPB source; this is noted as potentially underestimated given pork belly content, but the HPB canonical value is preserved. Fields set to -1 (data not available in HPB or USDA for this specific dish): vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g — detailed micronutrient and moisture data for this specific Singaporean braised glutinous rice preparation are not available in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of April 2026. GI of 72 is an analogous estimate based on glutinous rice GI data from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Oil Rice (油饭) as a composite dish has been published in peer-reviewed literature as of April 2026. Halal status is not confirmed as the dish typically contains pork belly and may use non-halal oyster sauce.
